coredata.dc:title: Significant enhancement of the photoelectrochemical activity of WO3 nanoflakes by carbon quantum dots decoration
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Weina
Zhang, Xiaofan
Brillet, Jérémie
Huang, Dekang
Li, Man
Wang, Mingkui
Shen, Yan
coredata.dc:description: This work reports on single-crystal WO3 nanoflakes decorated with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for efficient photoelectrochemical energy conversion system. The promising photoanodes can be fabricated via seed-mediated solvothermal method in combination with subsequent impregnation-assembling. A photocurrent density of 1.46 mA cm−2 at 1.0 V vs. Ag/AgCl can be achieved for the CQDs/WO3 electrode when performing linear sweep voltammetry measurements in 1 M H2SO4 solution under AM 1.5G illumination, which is about two times higher than that of the bare WO3 electrode. The enhancement of photoelectrochemical performance is ascribed to the enhanced light harvesting ability of CQDs and its accelerating charge transfer across the interface between CQDs/WO3 electrode and electrolyte. The improved activity indicates the potential of CQDs/WO3 as an efficient electrode for photoelectrochemical solar energy conversion.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial support from the 973 Program of China (Grants 2013CB922104 and 2014CB64350 ), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (HUST: 2016YXMS031 ), the NSFC ( 21103057 , 21173091 and 21450110418 ), CAS ( 2015PM055 ) and FNS ( P2ELP2_155350 ) are gratefully acknowledged. The authors thank the Analytical and Testing Centre of HUST and the Center of Micro-Fabrication and Characterization of WNLO for performing characterization of various samples. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HRTEM (inset) image of CQDs; (b) Size distributions of CQDs from multiple images. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The low (a) and high (b) magnification SEM images, TEM (c) and HRTEM (d) image of 1.93%CQDs/WO3 sample. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Cross sectional image of 1.93%CQDs/WO3 (a) and corresponding EDS mapping images of Sn (b), W (c) and C (d). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

XPS spectra of W 4f (a) and C1s (b) spectra for 1.93%CQDs/WO3 electrode; Raman spectrum of 1.93%CQDs/WO3 electrode (c); UV–vis absorption spectra for WO3 and 1.93%CQDs/WO3 electrodes (d). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Photoelectrochemical performances for WO3 and 1.93%CQDs/WO3 electrodes (a); IPCE spectrum (b) and magnified IPCE plots above 480 nm (the inset in Fig. 5b) for WO3 and 1.93%CQDs/WO3 electrodes at 1.0 V vs. Ag/AgCl. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
EIS Nyquist plots of WO3 and 1.93%CQDs/WO3 in the dark and under the illumination of AM 1.5G illumination. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Chopped amperometric i-t curves WO3 and 1.93%CQDs/WO3 electrodes at 1.0 V vs. Ag/AgCl. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Significant enhancement of the photoelectrochemical activity of WO3 nanoflakes by carbon quantum dots decoration

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Wuhan National Laboratory for Optoelectronics, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Luoyu Road 1037, Wuhan 430074, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Institute of Ceramics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 1295 Dingxi Road, Shanghai 200050, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work reports on single-crystal WO3 nanoflakes decorated with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for efficient photoelectrochemical energy conversion system. The promising photoanodes can be fabricated via seed-mediated solvothermal method in combination with subsequent impregnation-assembling. A photocurrent density of 1.46 mA cm−2 at 1.0 V vs. Ag/AgCl can be achieved for the CQDs/WO3 electrode when performing linear sweep voltammetry measurements in 1 M H2SO4 solution under AM 1.5G illumination, which is about two times higher than that of the bare WO3 electrode. The enhancement of photoelectrochemical performance is ascribed to the enhanced light harvesting ability of CQDs and its accelerating charge transfer across the interface between CQDs/WO3 electrode and electrolyte. The improved activity indicates the potential of CQDs/WO3 as an efficient electrode for photoelectrochemical solar energy conversion.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) solar energy conversion using semiconductor oxide electrodes provides a promising strategy to meet the global energy demands. A number of n-type metal oxide semiconductors have been widely investigated as photoanodes for PEC solar energy conversion, such as TiO2, ZnO, Fe2O3, BiVO4, and WO3 etc [1–5]. Among them, tungsten oxide (WO3) has received much attention due to its relatively narrow band gap (∼2.6 eV), highly positive valence band edge (3.0 eV vs. NHE), moderate hole diffusion length (∼150 nm), high electron mobility (∼12 cm2 V−1 s−1), low cost and abundance [6]. However, the PEC properties of WO3 photoelectrodes are still lower than the theoretical value (∼5 mA cm−2) under AM 1.5 G solar illumination, owning to the rapid electron–hole recombination, as well as the slow charge transfer at the semiconductor/electrolyte interface [7]. Additionally, WO3 is an indirect band gap semiconductor. Therefore, a thick film is needed for efficient light harvesting. However, a planar thick layer of photo-electrode material has low interfacial surface area and may cause serious electron–hole recombination, thus decrease its photo-activity [8]. Ongoing attempts devoted to overcome these issues include incorporation of surface oxygen-evolving catalyst [9,10], construction of a completely autonomous tandem cell and hetero-junction [11–13], element doping [14,15], nanostructure engineering [16–18] and so on. The nano-engineering technology has been employed to improve the efficiency of generation and separation of photo-generated electron–hole pairs. For example, electrodes with 2D nanosheet structures could provide a huge contact area with electrolytes and shorten the diffusion length for minority carriers, and thus reduce the recombination of photo-generated carriers [19–23]. In addition, materials such as carbon material, metal nanoparticles, and other semiconductor oxides have been used to decorate onto nanostructured photoelectrodes to synergistically improve the PEC activities [24–26].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new class of carbon nano-material [27], have attracted wide attention due to their facile and low cost synthesis, good photo-chemical stability as well as nontoxicity [28]. Notably, the semi-conducting CQDs could enhance light harvesting ability and accelerate interfacial charge transfer. Thus, an improved photo-activity would be expected for photoanodes decorated with CQDs [29]. Several CQDs/semiconductor composites have been reported for photo-catalytic water splitting and photo-degradation of organic pollutants [30–34].

In light of the excellent properties of CQDs and the limitations of WO3, a marriage of CQDs to the nanostructured WO3 may be an ideal strategy to develop efficient photo-electrodes for PEC solar energy conversion system. Herein, we show the finding of single-crystal porous WO3 nanoflakes decorated with CQDs via seed-mediated solvothermal method with subsequent impregnation-assembling. A significantly improved photocurrent was achieved for the CQDs/WO3 nanocomposite electrode in H2SO4 solution under simulated light. Furthermore, it is confirmed that the enhanced absorption ability and accelerating charge transfer across the electrode-electrolyte interface induced by CQDs are responsible for the enhanced PEC activity of CQDs/WO3 nanocomposites.

A seed-mediated solvothermal method was used to synthesize porous WO3 nanoflakes [17]. The seed solution was prepared by dissolving 1.25 g of H2WO4 and 0.5 g of poly(vinyl alcohol) into 17 mL of H2O2 (30 wt. %). WO3 seed layer was deposited on a F doped SnO2 (FTO) substrate by a spin coating process, followed by annealing at 500 C in air for 2 h. Another H2WO4 solution was prepared as follows: 2.5 g of H2WO4 and 34 mL of H2O2 (30 wt. %) were added into 50 mL of H2O, and the suspension was heated at 95 °C under stirring to form a clear solution, which was diluted to a concentration of 0.05 M for the solvothermal process. The solution for solvothermal synthesis contains 0.02 g of urea, 0.02 g of oxalic acid, 12.5 mL of acetonitrile, 0.5 mL of HCl (6 M) and 3 mL of H2WO4 solution (0.05 M). Solvothermal synthesis was carried out using a 20 mL Teflon-lined autoclave at 180 °C for 2 h, in which one seeded FTO was placed with the conducting side facing down against the wall of the Teflon liner. After cooling to room temperature, the substrate was washed with deionized water and dried with nitrogen flow, followed by annealing at 500 °C for 2 h in air with a heating rate of 2 °C min−1 to obtain crystalline monoclinic WO3.

CQDs were synthesized through a one-step alkali-assisted ultrasonic chemical method [35,36]. In a typical synthesis, 9.0 g of glucose was dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water in a glass beaker. Then an equal volume of NaOH solution (1 M) was added into the solution of glucose, followed by ultrasonic treatment (300 W, 40 KHz) for 4 h at room temperature. The raw solution obtained from glucose/NaOH was adjusted to pH = 7 with HCl, before a certain amount of ethanol was added drop by drop into the solution under continuous stirring.

An impregnation-assembling method was adopted for the synthesis of CQDs/WO3 composites [37]. Typically, the CQDs solutions with different concentrations were obtained by dilution and concentration. The as-synthesized WO3 photoanodes were immersed into the diluted, pristine and concentrated CQDs solutions for 50 min to obtain the CQDs/WO3 composites, and the amounts of CQDs for the composites were determined by elemental analysis, which were 0.81 wt%, 1.93 wt% and 2.28 wt% respectively. The CQDs/WO3 photoanodes were denoted as 0.81%CQDs/WO3, 1.93%CQDs/WO3, and 2.28%CQDs/WO3, respectively.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was carried out with X-ray diffractometer (X’ Pert PRO, PANalytical B.V.) to confirm the purity and crystallinity of the as-prepared samples using Cu Kα source radiation (1.540598 Å). Elemental analysis was performed using an elemental analyzer (Vario Micro cube, Germany). Chemical states of the sample were determined by X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS-ULTRA DLD-600W, Shimdzu) with a monochromatic Al Ka source operating at 150 W. Raman spectroscopy was carried out on a Raman spectrometer (LabRAM HR800) with a 532 nm excitation argon-ion laser as the excitation source. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Nova NanoSEM 450, FEI) equipped with an energy dispersion X-ray spectrometer (EDS) was used to investigate the morphology, element contents and spatial distribution of the samples. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image, high resolution transmission electron microscopy image (HRTEM) and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern were recorded on a transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai G2 F30, FEI). Diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were recorded on a UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-3600, Shimdzu) equipped with an integrating sphere in the region of 300–800 nm, and transformed to the UV–Visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra according to the Kubelka-Munk relationship.

Photoelectrochemical characterization was carried out with a CHI 630D electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, Shanghai, China) in a typical three-electrode quartz cell with CQDs/WO3, Ag/AgCl electrode and Pt wire as the working electrode, reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively. An aqueous solution of 1 M H2SO4 was used as the electrolyte, and high purity argon gas was purged to remove any dissolved oxygen before the measurements. Linear sweep voltammogram (LSV) was measured in the anodic direction at the scan rate of 10 mV s−1. A Xenon lamp (Newport, 69911) coupled with an AM 1.5 G filter was used as the light source, and the light intensity was set at 100 mW cm−2. Incident photon to current conversion efficiency (IPCE) was recorded at a potential of 1.0 V vs. Ag/AgCl employing the same light source with band-pass filters. A photometer was used to measure the intensity of the monochromatic light. The IPCE is evaluated using the equation of IPCE=(1240J ph)/(λP light), where J ph is the measured photocurrent density (mA cm−2) under the irradiation of monochromatic light; λ and P light are the wavelength (nm) and intensity (mW cm−2) of the monochromatic light, respectively. The evolved gas was measured using gas chromatography (Trustworthy Instrument, GC-2020N, China) with argon as a carrier gas. Mott-Schottky plot was performed at 1 kHz frequency in dark using an electrochemical impedance analyser (CHI 920C). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were carried out in dark and under illumination at a bias potential of 1.0 V vs. Ag/AgCl with an AC voltage magnitude of 5 mV amplitude.

Fig. 1 a and b present typical TEM image and particle size distribution histogram of the obtained CQDs. The high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image (the inset in Fig. 1a) depicts the crystal lattice spacing of around 0.231 nm, corresponding to the (100) facet of the graphite [38,39]. The obtained CQDs samples show monodisperse distribution with an average size of 2.0 ± 0.42 nm (Fig. 1b). Fig. S1 shows the absorption spectroscopy of CQDs. The peak in the region of 250–300 nm represents a typical absorption of an aromatic π system [40].

Fig. 2 a and b show the SEM images for the vertically aligned porous WO3 nanoflakes after coverage with CQDs (1.93 wt%). The nanoflakes with a thickness of approximately 30 nm were successfully grown on FTO substrates. Free space can be seen clearly among nanoflakes (Fig. 2b), which is helpful for the electrolyte penetration [8]. Moreover, the WO3 sample decorated with CQDs shows similar morphology with that of the bare WO3 sample (Fig. S2), probably due to the small size of CQDs (∼2 nm). The TEM image of 1.93%CQDs/WO3 sample (Fig. 2c) indicates the obtained porous nanoflake. Moreover, the HRTEM image (Fig. 2d) shows the lattice spacing of 0.38 and 0.36 nm, corresponding to the (002) and (200) facets of monoclinic WO3, respectively. The lattice spacing of 0.23 nm was observed corresponding to (100) facet of CQDs marked with red arrows in Fig. 2d, demonstrating the successful formation of 1.93%CQDs/WO3 nanocomposite. Furthermore, the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. S3) confirms the single-crystalline and monoclinic nature of porous WO3 nanoflakes [41,42].

Fig. 3 a shows the cross sectional image of 1.93%CQDs/WO3 film deposited onto the FTO substrate. As can be seen, the height of WO3 nanoflake is less than 2 μm with homogenously distribution of Sn, W, and C, as indicated with EDS mapping images in Fig. 3b–d. The cross sectional images of the WO3 electrode (Fig. S4) have ignorable change after decoration with CQDs (Fig. 3). In addition, XRD characterization was performed to investigate the crystal phase structure of the samples analysis for the 1.93%CQDs/WO3 sample (Fig. S5). The pristine WO3 can be indexed into monoclinic structure (JCPDS No. 00-043-1035), which was consisting with the HRTEM result. However, the characteristic peak for CQDs at 26° was absent in the XRD pattern probably due to a small quantity and relatively low diffraction intensity of CQDs in composites [29].

The elemental composition of 1.93%CQDs/WO3 nano-composite was further investigated with XPS characterization. Two peaks of the W4f spectra (Fig. 4 a) centered at 38.0 and 35.9 eV are assigned to W4f5/2 and W4f7/2, respectively, confirming the presence of WO3 [43]. The C1s XPS spectrum for the 1.93%CQDs/WO3 sample as presented in Fig. 4b confirms the presence of the CQDs. The peaks centered at 285.0, 286.6 and 288.3 eV are attributed to CC, CO and CO, respectively [44,45]. The O1s spectrum of 1.93%CQDs/WO3 (Fig. S6) have three peaks centered at 530.4, 532.0, and 533.1 eV, corresponding to WO, CO, and OH bonds, respectively. There is no WC peak (282.2 eV) existing in either W4f or C1s spectra, indicating that CQDs are integrated with WO3 rather than doped into the WO3 nanoflakes [46]. Fig. 4c shows the Raman spectrum of 1.93%CQDs/WO3. Two strong peaks at 715 and 806 cm−1 are ascribed to WO bending (δ) and WO stretching (ν) modes respectively, which are assigned to the typical Raman peaks of crystalline WO3. The weaker peaks at 272 and 326 cm−1 correspond to the bending δ (OWO) vibrations, while the peaks below 200 cm−1 are assigned to the lattice vibrations of WO3 [47,48]. In addition to the peaks from the bare WO3 nanoflakes, two additional typical peaks were observed for 1.93%CQDs/WO3. The peak centered at 1360 cm−1 (D band) corresponds to vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the termination plane of disordered graphite, while G band at 1580 cm−1 is assigned to the vibration of sp2-bonded carbon atoms in a two-dimensional (2D) hexagonal lattice [40]. Fig. 4d exhibits the absorption spectra of WO3 and 1.93%CQDs/WO3 films. WO3 nanoflakes film shows a strong absorbance in the short wavelength region of less than 460 nm. After coating with CQDs, the absorbance from 480 to 600 nm can be observed for the composite film.

EIS measurements were further performed to illustrate the origin of the high PEC activity. Fig. 6 shows the Nyquist plots of the WO3 and 1.93%CQDs/WO3 electrodes at 1.0 V vs. Ag/AgCl. The diameter of semicircle in the Nyquist plots reflects the electronic resistance for charge transfer across the interface between the electrode and the electrolyte. The diameter for the 1.93%CQDs/WO3 electrode is obviously smaller both in dark and under illumination conditions comparing with those of the WO3 electrode. This indicates that the introduction of CQDs into the WO3 electrode is helpful for a faster charge transfer across the electrode/electrolyte interface, and thus, resulting in a higher PEC activity [49]. This is in accordance with the improvement of photocurrent density in Fig. 5a. Furthermore, a qualitative comparison of donor densities was further made between the pristine WO3 and 1.93%CQDs/WO3 electrodes according to the Mott-Schottky plots (Fig. S9). The 1.93%CQDs/WO3 electrode showed a substantially smaller slope than that of the WO3 sample, suggesting significantly increased donor densities based on the Mott-Schottky equation Nd=(2/e 0 εε 0)[d(1/C 2)/dV]−1, where e 0 is the electronic charge value, ε
0 being the permittivity of the vacuum, ε being the relative permittivity of WO3, C being the space charge capacitance, N d being the donor density and V being the applied bias at the electrode. The enhanced donor density for the 1.93%CQDs/WO3 electrode facilitated the separation of electron–hole pairs and thus contributed to its higher photocurrent response.

Furthermore, we investigated the electrodes stability with chronoamperometry in 1 M H2SO4 solution at an imposed bias voltage of 1.0 V vs. Ag/AgCl under chopped AM 1.5G illumination. As shown in Fig. 7 , the bare WO3 electrode loses 42.7% of its initial photocurrent density after 1800 s. The deactivation may be due to the formation of peroxo species, which were observed for several n-type photoanodes such as Ca2Nb3O10, TiO2 and WO3 [50–52]. In contrast to the bare WO3 electrode, 1.93%CQDs/WO3 shows an improved photo-stability of approximately 84.8%. In addition, O2 generation was measured for 1.93%CQDs/WO3 using gas chromatography in 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte. However, we found that the 1.93%CQDs/WO3 electrode had negligible O2 production. Similar results have been observed previously [53]. In the case of photoelectrochemical solar energy conversion of WO3 in the presence of H2SO4 solution as discussed above, following reaction is suggested for photo-generated holes: SO4 2− + h+ → S2O8 2− [53]. Therefore, we employed Raman spectra to identify the existence of S2O8 2−. The photoelectrochemical reaction time was prolonged to 10 h to insure the detection of S2O8 2−. In addition, 1 M H2SO4 electrolytes containing with and without 0.1 M K2S2O8 were analyzed for comparison. As shown in Fig. S10, Raman characterization confirms the existence of S2O8 2− in electrolyte after continuous illumination, indicating the main product of S2O8 2− for the reaction onto the 1.93%CQDs/WO3 electrode surface. However, it is difficult to quantify this specie. Therefore, 0.05 mM of Ag2SO4 was added into 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte as a catalyst for the regeneration of SO4 2− from S2O8 2− with O2 evolution concomitantly. As shown in Fig. S11, O2 bubble was clearly observed on the edge of the working electrode after illumination. An amount of 4.54 μmol of O2 was evaluated after passing 2.01 C at 1.0 V vs. Ag/AgCl (Fig. S12). Therefore, the Faradic efficiency of O2 evolution was calculated to be 87%. This result indicates that the observed photo-current is real water splitting photocurrent, rather than due to photodegradation.

On the basis of the above experiments and analyses, we argue that the enhanced photocurrents of CQDs/WO3 electrodes can be attributed to following reasons. The introduction of semi-conducting CQDs in the composite causes more photo-generated electron–hole pairs than that of the WO3 electrode alone. The CQDs could enhance charge transfer at the electrode-electrolyte interface, and thus result in high PEC activity for the CQDs/WO3 electrodes.

In summary, we have successfully fabricated a porous WO3 nanoflakes electrode by a seed-mediated solvothermal method, followed by loading with CQDs through impregnation-assembling. The nanocomposite of CQDs/WO3 results in an obvious enhancement of photocurrent than that of bare WO3 for PEC solar energy conversion, which may be attributed to the enhanced light harvesting ability and accelerated interfacial charge transfer induced by CQDs. The above mentioned excellent properties of CQDs make it a promising material to enhance semiconductor’s activity for PEC energy conversion.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the 973 Program of China (Grants 2013CB922104 and 2014CB64350), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (HUST: 2016YXMS031), the NSFC (21103057, 21173091 and 21450110418), CAS (2015PM055) and FNS (P2ELP2_155350) are gratefully acknowledged. The authors thank the Analytical and Testing Centre of HUST and the Center of Micro-Fabrication and Characterization of WNLO for performing characterization of various samples.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2016.04.051.
